RePERS — where on the curve are you?

60-second positioning canvas for evaluators

2026-06-23· v0.1.1 shipped· main @ 5e7ce3c· repo: github.com/coolsocket/repers

⚡ What needs your input

  1. Which maturity tier (1–6) does your team / org sit in?
    Once you've picked a tier from the table below, leave a one-line comment in the D1 decision box at the bottom. That alone tells RePERS whether to adopt, route around, or wait.
Sections
A. Maturity curve B. Cross-repo handoff C. Router decision D. When to skip E. v0.1.1 status F. Verdict G. Decisions
📌 Executive summary

RePERS earns its keep at the upper half of the codebase maturity curve — scale-up, big-co, and cross-org settings where parallel AI agents need a contract to avoid clobbering each other and where evidence chains need to be re-verifiable across team / vendor / trust boundaries. At the lower half (greenfield, solo, single-domain) the harness is overhead the router will explicitly tell you to skip. The most under-told feature is the cross-repo handoff: release-pack.zip is a transferable, independently re-verifiable bundle — a feature most agent harnesses don't ship at all.

📊 A. The 6-tier maturity curve
Verdict colors: Skip (badge fail) — harness is overhead Selective (badge pend) — use for big changes only Adopt (badge pass) — sweet spot or higher
Tier Repo shape Verdict What you actually need
1. Day 0 — solo prototype (<1 k LOC) One file or two; sketching No — naked agent wins A chat window
2. Early product (1–3 devs, 1–10 k LOC, single domain) Handful of files, light tests Almost always no CI + pre-commit lint
3. Growing product (5–10 devs, 50 k LOC, 2–3 domains) Multi-file PRs common; conflicts start Selectively — big changes only (migrations, sweeps, refactors) Branch protection + structured code review
4. Scale-up (20+ engineers, 200 k+ LOC monorepo) Parallel feature work daily; multi-agent helping multiple engineers Yes — sweet spot target_files isolation + dispatch contract to prevent N agents clobbering
5. Big company / regulated (100+ engineers, multi-service, audit trails) Cross-cutting work needs evidence chains; multi-vendor agents Yes — as lingua franca Standard contract across agent fleets + portable audit trail
6. Cross-org / OSS ecosystem (multi-repo deps, vendor diversity) Agent in repo A produces something repo B has to verify Yes — for the handoff Contract that survives vendor + org + trust boundaries

Pattern: at the small end the overhead dominates; at the large end the coordination IS the work, and absence of a contract IS the pain. The harness feels cheaper as the codebase gets bigger.

🔗 B. Cross-repo / cross-team handoff primitives
Cross-repo flow RePERS primitive What it proves
Team A produces a release artifact; Team B's CI must consume + audit release-pack.zip (transferable archive: install + readiness + evidence + bootstrap + benchmark + state) A single signed zip moves between repos / orgs / clouds without losing audit context
Team B receives the pack; their AI/CI must verify independently release-pack-verify --archive <pack> --json Receiver re-verifies checksums + manifest + embedded evidence WITHOUT trusting sender's vendor or JSON
Fresh repo wants to adopt from a pack receiver-fixture --json + source-install-fixture --json Fresh git init + 1 command → working .repers/ runtime; both contracts proven in CI
Two repos cooperate via a temporary bare remote (air-gapped review) publish-clone-fixture --json Source pushes to bare remote → clone re-verifies → no network deps
A different LLM vendor (Codex / Gemini / your own) drives a lane dispatch.v1 manifest + step_result.v1 artifact Contract is JSON-in / JSON-out; worker doesn't need to know which vendor the supervisor uses
🧭 C. Router decision tree (6 outcomes)
skip
Task is trivial; harness is pure overhead.
(none)
Speedup: 0.2× (overhead)
R-only
Spike / scoping; decide before building.
R
Speedup: n/a (preserves context, no execution)
R-S
Docs / config only; skip execute.
R → S
Speedup: ~1.0× (lightweight)
R-E-R
Hotfix / single-file; test pins the answer.
R → E → R
Speedup: ~0.5–0.8× (modest ceremony cost)
R-P-E-R
Multi-file in one domain.
R → P → E → R
Speedup: ~1.5–2.0× (parallel lanes pay)
R-P-E-R-S
Multi-domain, multi-day, multi-agent.
R → P → E → R → S
Speedup: ~2.0–3.0× (full chain pays)
python .repers/scripts/repers.py route \
  --task "<your task description>" \
  --est-files <N> \
  --json
🩺 D. Calibration — when to skip the harness
Use the router; small tasks should skip

For a single-file, single-function fix — especially when the failing test pins the expected output — the harness's coordination overhead costs more than it saves. There's nothing for the parallel-dispatch + JSON-handoff layer to coordinate over one work-item.

Recommendation: always invoke /repers-route first. For trivial tasks it returns next_step.action: "naked_loop" and tells the calling agent to use its own Edit/Read/Bash. The harness's value scales with multi-file, multi-domain, multi-agent work — see Section A.

📦 E. v0.1.1 shipment status
25
capabilities (was 24; +1 route)
5
skills (was 4; +1 /repers-route)
43
installed files
🟢 green
CI (ubuntu / windows / macos)
45 s
e2e walkthrough: real run, every CLI command executed
2
UX fixes from dogfood: review auto-refreshes plan.json; verify-install emits actionable hint
✅ F. Verdict
Pursue if you're tier 4–6; route around if 1–3

The harness is opinionated. It will tell you to skip when it doesn't fit (the router routes the sqlfluff bug to "naked agent is faster"). When it fits — parallel multi-agent work on a real codebase with cross-team handoffs — there is no comparable open contract layer in the agentic-AI ecosystem today. LangGraph / CrewAI / OpenHands are runtimes that execute LLM calls. RePERS is what sits above any of them so an N-agent fleet can carve up real engineering work without clobbering, hand off with evidence, and ship verifiably.

Tradeoff to call out plainly: the cross-repo handoff story (Section B) is the strongest under-told piece, but it presumes the receiving team also has RePERS installed — otherwise the pack is just a zip. v0.2 will reduce that adoption friction.

📝 G. Decisions
D1 · Which maturity tier (1–6) am I in?

Pick a row from Section A — one line of context.

D2 · Do we have parallel multi-agent or multi-team work today?

If yes, the dispatch contract is the immediate value; if no, the router will keep saying "skip".

D3 · Do we need auditable handoffs between teams / vendors / repos?

If yes, the release-pack story (Section B) is the strongest fit. If your team is single-vendor and single-org today, that part is optional.
